22: sy ei ho huios mou ho agapetos, en soi eudokesa — you are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased — Luke's baptism declaration is identical to Mark's; a variant reading (D manuscript) gives you are my Son, today I have begotten you, quoting Psalms 2:7 directly. The standard text combines Psalms 2:7 (my Son) with Genesis 22:2 (agapetos = only/beloved son) and Isaiah 42:1 (with you I am well pleased = my chosen one in whom my soul delights). The triplicity of the title signals the depth of the Christological identification.
1: en etei de pentekaidekaton tēs hēgemonian Tiberiou Kaisaros — in the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar — Luke's synchronistic dating, using six concurrent rulers, is the most elaborate historical synchronism in the NT and follows Thucydidean historiographical method. The fifteenth year of Tiberius (who began sole reign in 14 CE) places John's ministry at approximately 28-29 CE. The list: Tiberius (emperor), Pilate (prefect of Judea), Herod Antipas (tetrarch of Galilee), Philip (tetrarch of Ituraea-Trachonitis), Lysanias (tetrarch of Abilene), Annas and Caiaphas (high priests). The two high priests reflect the political reality that Rome had replaced the Hasmonean-era single high priest with appointees.
2: egeneto rēma theou epi Iōannēn — the word of God came upon John — ginomai rēma theou epi is the standard LXX formula for prophetic commissioning (cf. Jeremiah 1:1-2; Ezekiel 1:3; Hosea 1:1; Joel 1:1; Micah 1:1); Luke presents John's calling as formally equivalent to the classical OT prophets. En tē erēmō (in the wilderness) is the prophetic staging location — John is commissioned in the wilderness, the same space as Moses at the burning bush and Elijah at Horeb.
3: baptisma metanoias eis aphesin hamartiōn — a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins — the phrase is dense: baptisma (a dipping, submersion — not merely sprinkling); metanoia (change of mind/orientation, turning — from meta = after/change + nous = mind; in LXX it translates shuv, the covenantal turning-back to God); eis aphesin hamartiōn (for/unto the release/forgiveness of sins — aphesis from aphiēmi = to release, let go, forgive). The preposition eis is hotly debated: it can indicate purpose (for the purpose of forgiveness) or result (resulting in forgiveness), affecting whether the baptism is a sign of a prior repentance or an instrument of forgiveness.
4: phōnē boōntos en tē erēmō — a voice of one crying in the wilderness — the LXX text of Isaiah 40:3 that Luke quotes positions the comma differently from the MT: "a voice of one crying: in the wilderness prepare the way" vs. "a voice of one crying in the wilderness: prepare the way." Luke follows the LXX, connecting the voice to the wilderness (John is in the wilderness, Luke 3:2) rather than the way. Ktl: Luke's Isaiah citation (Luke 40:3-5) is the longest of the Synoptic quotations of this passage, extending to "all flesh will see the salvation of God" — the Gentile-inclusive conclusion that Matthew and Mark omit.
5: pasa pharagx plērōthēsetai kai pan oros kai bounos tapeinōthēsetai — every valley will be filled, every mountain and hill brought low — the quotation continues Isaiah 40:4-5 in LXX form; Luke extends the citation to include the universalist conclusion. The road-building imagery (eutheias = straight, tracheias = rough) is Isaiah's metaphor for divine preparation of the way for the returning glory of YHWH. The straightening and leveling is both literal (road construction for royal processions) and metaphorical (the removal of obstacles to divine visitation).
6: kai opsetai pasa sarx to sōtērion tou theou — and all flesh will see the salvation of God — pasa sarx (all flesh) is the universalist formula for humanity without ethnic distinction; sōtērion (salvation, noun form) rather than sōtēria is Luke's preferred form (also Luke 2:30; Acts 28:28). Luke's extension of the Isaiah citation to this verse is programmatic: his Gospel and Acts will trace the fulfillment of the "all flesh" scope of salvation. The salvation is to be seen (opsetai) — Luke's emphasis on witnesses who see is characteristic (Luke 1:2; Acts 1:8).
7: genēmata echidnōn — offspring/brood of vipers — the noun genēmata (things born, brood) from ginomai rather than gennēmata from gennaō (to beget); the distinction is between "what has been born" and "what has been begotten." Some manuscripts have gennēmata (cf. Matthew 3:7). Echidna (viper) is one of the most venomous snakes in the Levant; the address "offspring of vipers" carries the resonance of the serpent of Gen 3 — John is accusing the crowd of being offspring of the ancient adversary. Tis hypedeicen hymin phugein — who warned you to flee — hypodeiknymi (show under, warn) is used of prophetic or divine warning (cf. Acts 9:16; Luke 20:35).
8: poiēsate oun karpous axious tēs metanoias — therefore produce fruits worthy of repentance — the agricultural metaphor of fruit as evidence of genuine inner change is standard in prophetic rhetoric (Isaiah 5:1-7; Jeremiah 17:8). The command is plural (poiēsate — all of you, collectively) addressed to the crowd. mē arxēsthe legein en heautois — do not begin to say to yourselves — archomai with the infinitive is a Lukan idiom for beginning an action; the warning not to begin a thought implies that this rationalizing has already started. ek tōn lithōn toutōn — from these stones — the possible Aramaic wordplay (avanim/stones = banim/sons) works in Aramaic but not Greek, suggesting an underlying Aramaic tradition.
9: ēdē de kai hē axinē pros tēn rhizan tōn dendrōn keitai — even now the axe is laid to the root of the trees — ēdē (even now, already) creates urgency; the axe is not yet swinging but is positioned. Axinē (axe) is the chopping implement for felling trees, used as a judgment metaphor throughout the prophets (Isaiah 10:15; Jeremiah 46:22). The fruit/root/axe metaphor is coherent: trees that produce no fruit have their root-existence threatened. Pan de dendron mē poioun karpon kalon — every tree not bearing good fruit — the singular universal (pan... mē) addresses each hearer individually.
10: kai epērōtōn auton hoi ochloi — the crowds were asking him — epērōtaō (ask, question) combined with the imperfect (epērōtōn) suggests repeated or ongoing questioning — the crowd continues to ask. The three-group structure (crowd, tax collectors, soldiers) gives John's ethical instruction a social breadth that covers the economic (sharing), the systemic (tax justice), and the coercive (military/police power) dimensions of the community's life.
11: ho echōn duo chitōnas metadotō tō mē echonti — the one who has two tunics, let him share with the one who has none — chitōn (inner garment, tunic) is the basic garment worn under the outer himation; to have two was modest prosperity, to have none was poverty. Metadidōmi (share, give a portion from one's own) is Luke's verb for practical generosity (Romans 1:11; Ephesians 4:28). The food instruction (brōmata = food) follows the same pattern: share from what you have. John's ethical teaching is not revolutionary redistribution but basic covenant solidarity.
12: elthon de kai telōnai baptisthēnai — tax collectors also came to be baptized — telōnēs (tax collector) describes those who bid for the right to collect imperial taxes in a region and were despised as collaborators with Rome and as systemically dishonest. Their coming for baptism signals that John's call includes those outside the social-religious mainstream. Didaskalos, ti poiēsōmen — teacher, what shall we do — the honorific didaskalos (teacher) from the tax collectors signals their genuine inquiry.
13: mēden pleon para to diatetagmenon hymin prassete — collect nothing more than what you have been authorized — diatetagmenon (ordered, authorized — perfect passive participle of diatassō) refers to the officially sanctioned collection rate. Tax collectors regularly charged above the official rate and pocketed the difference; John commands ethical compliance with the legal framework. He does not condemn the profession but its abuse — a notable moderation compared to the revolutionary expectations he might have encouraged.
14: mēdena diaseiēte mēde sykophantēsēte — do not intimidate anyone or extort falsely — diaseiō (shake thoroughly, intimidate) is specifically the extortion by force or threat; sykophantēsēte (from sykophantēs) denotes false accusation for personal gain, extortion by false charges. The soldiers' ethics (strateuomenoi = serving as soldiers, possibly including police/enforcement roles) are addressed with the same concrete specificity as the tax collectors. Arkeisthe tois opsōniois hymin — be content with your wages — opsōnion (soldier's pay, rations) is military pay vocabulary.
15: prosdokōntos de tou laou kai dialogizomenōn pantōn en tais kardiais autōn peri tou Iōannou — while the people were in expectation, all reasoning in their hearts about John — prosdokaō (expect, await) describes the messianic anticipation that John's ministry had awakened; the people were wondering whether he himself was the Christ. Dialogizomai en kardiais (reason in hearts) is Luke's phrase for the interior deliberation that is not yet spoken aloud (cf. Luke 1:29; Luke 5:22; Luke 12:17).
16: baptizō hymas hydati, erchetai de ho ischuroteros mou — I baptize you with water, but one who is mightier than I is coming — the contrast is water/pneumati hagiō kai puri (Holy Spirit and fire). The single preposition en governing both pneumati hagiō and puri (Holy Spirit and fire) suggests these are one unified baptism rather than two separate ones — the Spirit-fire baptism is the single eschatological event. Ou ouk eimi hikanos lysai ton himanta tōn hypodēmatōn autou — whose sandal strap I am not worthy to untie — the untying of sandals was the task of the lowest slave; John places himself below even slave rank relative to the Coming One.
17: hou to ptuon en tē cheiri autou — whose winnowing fork is in his hand — ptuon (winnowing fork, shovel) is the flat wooden implement used to toss threshed grain into the wind so the lighter chaff is blown away and the heavier grain falls back. The threshing floor (alōn) metaphor is a standard prophetic judgment image (Isaiah 41:16; Jeremiah 15:7; Micah 4:12). Achuron (chaff) vs. siton (wheat/grain) — the separation of chaff and grain is the judgment that the Coming One will execute. Puri asbstō — unquenchable fire — the eschatological fire of judgment.
18: polla men oun kai hetera parakalōn euēngelizeto ton laon — with many other exhortations he was preaching good news to the people — parakalōn (exhorting, encouraging — present participle of parakaleō) is one of Luke's favorite verbs for the proclamation that calls to action. Euēngelizeto (imperfect of euangelizō) — Luke's distinctive proclamation-verb characterizes even John's preaching as "good news" proclamation, though the content is primarily repentance and warning rather than kingdom arrival.
19: ho de Hērōdēs ho tetrarchēs — Herod the tetrarch — Luke introduces John's imprisonment prolepticly (before the baptism narrative is complete) to clear the Herodian complication from the narrative. Elenkhomenos hyp autou (being reproved by him) — the participle of elenchō (reprove, expose) describes John's confrontation of Herod over Herodias (his brother Philip's wife, Leviticus 18:16) and his other crimes (ponēria).
20: prosēthēken kai touto epi pasin — he added this also to all of them — the phrase frames Herod's imprisonment of John as the culmination of a series of ponērias (evil deeds) that Luke summarizes without detailing. The proleptic imprisonment notice removes Herod from the baptism scene — Luke will not have Herod present at the baptism as Matthew and Mark do not either.
21: en tō baptisthēnai hapanta ton laon kai Iēsou baptisthentos kai proseuchomenou — when all the people were being baptized and when Jesus was baptized and was praying — three notable Lukan features: (1) Jesus's baptism is subordinated grammatically (Iēsou baptisthentos — aorist passive participle, genitive absolute, not the main verb); (2) the main verb is proseuchomenou (while praying — present participle); (3) the heavens open specifically in response to prayer. Luke's prayer emphasis places the baptism-theophany within Jesus's prayer life — the revelation comes while he prays.
23: hōsei etōn triakonta archomenos — about thirty years old when he began — hōsei (about, approximately) is Luke's qualification that this is not a precise number; thirty was the traditional age of priestly service (Numbers 4:3) and of Davidic kingship (2 Samuel 5:4). Archomenos (beginning — present participle, perhaps nominative absolute) signals the inception of the public ministry. Hōs enomizeto (as was supposed/thought) — the passive of nomizō (think customary, suppose) signals that what follows is the legal genealogy, not the biological one.
24: Luke's genealogy runs from Jesus back to Adam in ascending order (unlike Matthew's descending from Abraham), which serves Luke's universal theological purpose: he is tracing humanity's origin, not merely Israel's Davidic line. The names between Joseph and Zerubbabel differ significantly from Matthew's list, which has generated extensive discussion; the most likely explanation is that Luke records Mary's biological line (through Heli = Mary's father) while Matthew records Joseph's legal Davidic line — the genealogy is Joseph's in both cases legally, but Luke's biological connections may trace Mary's ancestry through different branches.
25: Luke's genealogy from Mattathias through Cosam and Addi covers the post-exilic period with names not found in the OT or Matthew, suggesting access to tribal/family records preserved into the Second Temple period. The genealogical precision — with 77 names from Adam to Jesus (symbolic of completeness, 7×11) — indicates that Luke is presenting a carefully structured theological genealogy, not merely a historical list.
26: The name sequence through Maath, Mattathias, and Semein in Luke's genealogy is part of the post-exilic segment that diverges from Matthew. Luke's genealogy through Nathan (son of David, Luke 3:31) rather than Solomon (Matthew's line) may reflect the use of 2 Samuel 5:14 / 1 Chronicles 3:5; Zechariah's prophecy that the house of Nathan would mourn (Zechariah 12:12) is sometimes cited as relevant to the messianic line.
27: Luke's genealogy passes through Zerubbabel (the Davidic governor who led the return from exile) and Shealtiel — names also in Matthew's genealogy at this point — before diverging again. The convergence at Zerubbabel/Shealtiel is the one consistent point where the two genealogies agree after David; from here both traditions required connection to the Davidic line through the post-exilic community.
28: Luke's genealogy continues its careful enumeration through the Davidic line; the names in this portion (Cosam, Addi, Melchi, etc.) are not cross-referrable to OT records, which is consistent with post-exilic family records that were maintained within priestly and royal families for purposes of covenant inheritance claims.
29: The continuation through Elmadam, Er, and Joshua in Luke's genealogy maintains the careful tracing of the Davidic line. Luke's genealogy contains 77 names from Adam to Jesus — a number that is likely deliberate (seven sets of eleven, or seven being the number of completeness), reflecting Luke's theological shaping of the historical material he received.
30: Luke's genealogy through Simeon, Levi, and Matthat covers the pre-exilic portion with additional Davidic line names. The presence of Levi and Simeon as ancestors of Jesus (not the patriarchs but later figures) is consistent with the common practice of naming children after famous ancestors; multiple generations might share the same names, creating the appearance of conflation with earlier figures.
31: tou Nathan tou Dauid — son of Nathan, son of David — Luke's genealogy traces the Davidic descent through Nathan (2 Samuel 5:14; 1 Chronicles 3:5) rather than Solomon (Matthew's line). Both Nathan and Solomon were sons of David by Bathsheba; the divergence at this point explains the different name lists in the two genealogies. Zechariah 12:12 mentions the "house of Nathan" separately, and this Nathan-branch was a recognized alternative Davidic line.
32: tou Iessai tou Iōbēd tou Boos — son of Jesse, son of Obed, son of Boaz — this section of Luke's genealogy converges with Matthew's (and with Ruth 4:17-22): Jesse-Obed-Boaz-Salmon. Both genealogies must pass through the same pre-Davidic figures; the differences between Matthew and Luke are concentrated in the post-Davidic and post-exilic periods. Boaz and Ruth's union (the Moabitess) is the Gentile inclusion precedent in the Davidic ancestry.
33: tou Aminadab tou Admin tou Arni — Luke's genealogy includes Arni (not in Matthew) between Hezron and Aminadab; Matthew has Ram (= Arni in Greek transliteration of the same Hebrew name) directly. The slight variations in transliteration of Hebrew names between Matthew and Luke reflect independent Greek translations of the same Aramaic/Hebrew sources.
34: tou Iakōb tou Isaak tou Abraam — son of Jacob, son of Isaac, son of Abraham — Luke reaches the patriarchs at this point; from here to Adam the genealogy follows the LXX Septuagint genealogies (Gen 5; 11). Matthew stops at Abraham; Luke continues to Adam and God, serving his universal theological purpose. The three patriarchs are the covenant foundation — the promises to Abraham, confirmed through Isaac and Jacob, now reach their fulfillment in Jesus.
35: Luke's genealogy continues through the pre-Abrahamic patriarchs in descending order: Peleg, Reu, Serug, Nahor, Terah — the Table of Nations and primeval genealogy of Genesis 11:10-26. Luke follows the LXX enumeration, which adds Cainan (son of Arphaxad) in Luke 3:36 — a name present in the LXX of Genesis 11:12-13 but absent from the MT.
36: tou Kainam tou Arphaxad — son of Cainan, son of Arphaxad — the Cainan between Shem and Arphaxad is present in the LXX of Genesis 11:12-13 and Luke's genealogy follows this reading. The MT lacks Cainan here; the LXX addition (possibly from Genesis 10:24) creates a textual difference between the Hebrew and Greek OT. Luke's use of the LXX Cainan is consistent with his broader dependence on the LXX for OT citations throughout Luke-Acts.
37: Luke's genealogy continues through the ante-diluvian patriarchs: Methuselah, Enoch, Jared, Mahalaleel, Cainan — following Gen 5 in LXX order. Enoch is particularly significant: Genesis 5:24 notes that "Enoch walked with God and was no more, because God took him away" — a cryptic reference to translation without death that generated extensive Second Temple literature (1 Enoch, 2 Enoch). The presence of Enoch in the lineage of Jesus connects the eschatological traditions of Enoch's translation to the ultimate eschatological figure.
38: tou Sēth tou Adam tou theou — son of Seth, son of Adam, son of God — Luke's genealogy ends where it began, with the divine: the son of Adam, son of God that closes the genealogy frames Jesus as the completion of what Adam began. Adam was the son of God by creation; Jesus is the Son of God by eternal generation and incarnation. Where Adam failed the sonship (the temptation narrative immediately follows in Luke 4:1-13), Jesus will succeed. The genealogy that began with "son of God" (in the baptism voice, Luke 3:22) ends with "son of God" (the first man's relationship to his Creator) — the inclusio structures Luke's understanding of what the incarnation accomplishes: the restoration of humanity's original relationship to God, realized in the Son who is both the Father's eternal Son and Adam's descendant.
